deconstruction_targets: the Church's leniency and attempts at reconciliation
the possibility of repentance after conviction
constructive_proposals: arranging for zealous men to console the prisoner and induce confession
imprisoning a penitent heretic for life if he confesses out of fear of death
allowing secular courts to moderate the sentence of death

scapegoat.name: N.
scapegoat.justification: N. is portrayed as the embodiment of heretical defiance, having persistently refused to repent despite prolonged efforts by Church authorities to induce confession and reconciliation; his continued denial and refusal to appear are framed as moral failures that justify both ecclesiastical condemnation and handover to secular justice.

moral_justification: The Church positions itself as acting out of duty to preserve doctrinal purity, protect the faithful from heresy through example, and uphold divine justice; N.'s refusal to repent after repeated opportunities is presented as evidence of moral corruption that necessitates severe punitive measures, including excommunication and delivery to secular authorities.
summary: This text presents a deconstructive rhetorical framework in which the Church meticulously documents its procedural adherence while condemning N. as an irredeemable heretic whose defiance justifies both ecclesiastical expulsion and handover to secular justice; although it includes limited constructive mechanisms like confession incentives and conditional leniency, these are framed as last resorts rather than genuine reconciliatory efforts, reinforcing a tone of moral superiority and finality.
